planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring once soil is workable and replant divisions at the same depth in consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant Hart's Pennyroyal in moist to seasonally wet soil along pond edges, rain gardens, or stream margins where its narrow, peppermint-scented foliage can spread freely. Divide clumps every two to three years in spring to maintain vigor and prevent overcrowding. Harvest stems for culinary or herbal use by cutting back to just above a leaf node, which encourages bushy regrowth. Although more drought-tolerant than common pennyroyal, it performs best when soil moisture remains consistent through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seeds require light to germinate so press onto the soil surface without covering.

directions: Scatter seeds on the surface of moist, humus-rich soil in a shaded or dappled-light location in fall, pressing them lightly so they make good contact without being buried. Plants spend their first season establishing a root system and will not produce their tall, branching flower spikes until the second year. Once established, Maryland Figwort self-sows reliably and requires little maintenance beyond removing spent stems in late winter. Site it toward the back of a native planting where its bold height and wildlife value shine without its modest flowers needing close inspection.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow onto moist seed-starting mix and do not cover, as seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last expected frost by pressing them onto the surface of moist seed-starting mix under grow lights, maintaining soil temperatures around 65 degrees Fahrenheit. Transplant seedlings outdoors after frost danger has passed into deep, fertile, consistently moist soil in full sun. Plants grow slowly in their first year but develop into bold architectural clumps by the second season, sending up whorled-leaf stems topped with pink to lilac candelabra flower spikes in midsummer. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to maintain vigor and share this underused pollinator plant with other gardeners.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in spring once temperatures are consistently warm; treat cut ends with rooting hormone and maintain high humidity until roots establish.

directions: Take semi-hardwood cuttings in spring, treat with rooting hormone, and root in a warm, humid environment with bottom heat around 75 to 80 degrees Fahrenheit until a strong root system develops before transplanting. Choose a site with full sun, good drainage, and ample space, as this large tree can reach 20 to 30 feet tall and wide at maturity in frost-free climates. Water regularly during the first two years to establish deep roots, then reduce irrigation as the tree becomes more self-sufficient during dry periods. In zone 9, plant in a sheltered microclimate and mulch heavily at the base to protect roots if a rare frost threatens, as even brief freezes can cause significant dieback.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown specimens in early spring before bud break; cuttings root slowly and are best attempted in summer with mist propagation.

directions: Take softwood to semi-hardwood cuttings in early to midsummer, treat with rooting hormone, and root under intermittent mist or in a high-humidity enclosure, as magnolia cuttings root slowly and benefit from bottom heat around 70 degrees Fahrenheit. Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in early spring in a sheltered location with full sun and slightly acidic, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil, setting the root flare at or just above grade. Avoid planting in frost pockets, as the large, showy flowers that emerge before foliage in late winter and early spring are highly susceptible to late-season frost damage. Minimal pruning is needed; remove only dead or crossing branches immediately after flowering to preserve the natural, multi-stemmed form that makes this tree so striking.
